Looking at this code now I see that we have a possibility for a problem down the road.  Right now you are sending the `params` to be processed by `python_to_couch` function and right now it skips `keys` and does not perform any translation or conversion to the `keys` key or its value.  So you can get away with doing this `json.dumps` statement here.  If at any point the `python_to_couch` code changes and begins to process `keys` then you may run into a situation where you are performing multiple `json.dumps` commands on `keys`.

I know that you moved the code as it was originally but I think that had this same flaw.  This is probably a good time to correct that flaw and ensure that you pop off `keys` from `params` and handle `keys` as you do here and then pass `params` to `python_to_couch`.

OK, will refactor with `()` indeed :+1:. The use cases for using the default keys can be:

- a broken (custom or in-house) instrumentation which is now setting the service early enough. In that case it has no way to guess what rate to apply, because when they are submitted later, the spans do have a service set. So the idea here is to pick the default value returned by the agent, which is the global sampling rate. Technically, it causes over-sampling (dropping too many traces), which is not great, but not as bad as using `1` and send everything.
- an application that is starting and/or is having some service name change (services could be defined dynamically according to some variable flag, who knows, or some services could show up rarely and appear "some time" after the other) so when a new service shows up, instead of using 1 we use the default sampling rate (this is done here: https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/pull/359/files#diff-073d17b74132da4920e45dbb8d5de860R88) which at least is a little context dependant, if we're already at `0.01` we won't "jump" to `1` just because the service is new.

Globally, it's really about providing a default behavior that is a little context dependant, it's not the right rate (this, we only now later) but at least we get a reasonable hint on what to do.
The pass is so that we don't fall through to the `else` condition.

Let's make this slightly more than "all numbers from 1 to N, each occurring exactly once".

I would create a fixture that represents the list of numbers for that test run. Parameterize it so that you try the tests against different collections of numbers. Try to have at least the following

- What you have right now. Just 1000 numbers with no duplication.
- 100 random numbers between 1 and 1000 (so that there are many duplicates).
- Numbers from opposite ends -- some very small numbers, some very large numbers.

Then in the tests where you're doing `create_values(n)`, just use that fixture instead. The test will be run multiple times with the different lists.

See http://pytest.org/latest/example/parametrize.html#indirect-parametrization-with-multiple-fixtures.

Two underscores in Python signifies a _private_ member, visible only by instances of this _exact_ class but no subclasses. When classes are not really meant to be extended, and the possibility of overriding these private methods is not taken into account or well thought through, I find such usage ambiguous and even dangerous.

This code implies, first, that the `LDA` class is potentially meant to be extended by subclasses, and second, that the `__topics_words` method needs to be exactly this. So if functionality ever needs to be overriden, wherever this method is _used_, that whole other method (this method's caller) has to be overriden (as opposed to only this method if it were started with a single underscore (`_topics_words`) and be marked _protected_.

The top-voted answers here are relevant: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/797771/python-protected-attributes
I'm sure there's more wisdom to be found on why avoid "private private" members in Python, C++, Java, or altogether.

Presumably the order here is going to be the same as it is displayed in on PyPi?

The user will probably want to present a certain order that is not alphabetical. If the config is in code (i.e. setup.py) the user will have to make sure this preserves ordering (i.e. use a collections.OrderedDict, or dict on Python3.6+).

However, in the setup.cfg the dict creation is done by setuptools, and it isn't ordered. As python2.6 is not supported anymore, we can replace [the dictionary creation](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/1210/files#diff-360050608fe825a457c8093298edf207R219) with a collections.OrderedDict to preserve the order.

A, ok, I think that is a common misunderstanding of setuptools and dependency declaration in Python Eggs. 
http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#declaring-dependencies
describe that only install_requires and extra_requires are used, if declared by external packages or pip install. Also only those are reflected in the *.egg-info/requires.txt file. 
setup_requires is only evaluated if 

    python setup.py install

or 

    python setup.py develop 

is used (--> see http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#new-and-changed-setup-keywords setup_requires).

I'm not sure I understand your requested change- Theano handles creation of bilinear upsample filters within `bilinear_upsampling`, it's not an argument passed into `bilinear_upsampling` by the change in the PR.

The second input to `bilinear_upsampling` is the scalar scale factor to upsample the tensor by, not the filters used for convolution as it would be with something like `T.nnet.conv2d()`.

The output of `bilinear_upsampling` is a 4D tensor of dims `[batchsize, num_filters, row * scale_factor, col*scale_factor]` , where `num_filters` is the same as the number of filters of the previous layer- only the dims of the two trailing axes are affected by `bilinear_upsampling`.

Please do correct me if I'm wrong about any of the above, any feedback is welcome.

int spillback_count = execution_spec->getSpillbackCount();
